title: Some (more) thoughts on Dungeon Crusade, The Maze of Peril, and Engor's Sword Arm
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LXW_Z8rp3bU

A scattered, fond mid-campaign update from Daniel (@dungeondive). For the first time he has the full Dungeon Crusade rig set up on the dining table — not the hobby room — with the village and dungeon UI sideboards in their proper positions, played in fragments across an entire week off work. The Quick Reference Guide makes the relearn easy. Surprisingly, with everything sprawled across six feet of table, he confirms he still prefers his small-table binder system: easier to reach, harder to lose chits in the sea of components, and his paper character sheets beat the slider-clip stat trackers.

He frames the play session against two parallel reads. **The Maze of Peril** by John Eric Holmes (1986) — the doctor better known as the editor of the basic D&D blue-box rulebook — is, on Daniel's reading, the most dungeon-crawl novel he has ever held: Boinger the halfling fighter, Murray the mage, a dark elf, and a layered mega-labyrinth nobody knows the depth of. **Engor's Sword Arm** by David C. Smith is a chap book DMR Books rescued from a forgotten warehouse stash of unsold Forgotten Ages Press copies — a late-90s sword-and-sorcery piece by a Robert E. Howard-circle veteran.

Two house rules he's running: **all junk loot pulled** from the deck (treasure-chest disappointment isn't fun), and a **trap-engagement reward** (two life force for stepping onto a flipped non-trap, two for evading damage on a real one) so the heroes actually interact with Dean Spencer's art instead of routing around it.

----

Daniel cuts the junk loot deck out entirely as a house rule. Is anti-reward design (junk, blanks, "big thank yous") doing real work in a treasure deck, or is it just padding the disappointment column?
